text.title: Schedule Development Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the schedule-development methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Use FS dependencies by default; SS/FF/SF only when truly required and document the justification.
rule.rationale: Most 'we can parallelize' claims fail at integration; FS is conservative and explicit.

rule.statement: Track near-critical paths (float < 2 days) alongside the critical path; report them at every status review.
rule.rationale: A near-critical path one day shorter becomes critical after one slip and is invisible without slack analysis.

rule.statement: Lock the schedule baseline after kickoff; re-baseline only on approved change requests, never on standup updates.
rule.rationale: Constant re-baselining hides slip from stakeholders.

rule.statement: Effort hours are not duration days; model resource availability explicitly (40 effort-hours at 50% availability = 10 days).
rule.rationale: Effort-vs-duration confusion is the most common single source of schedule slip in agent-generated schedules.

rule.statement: Reject any schedule where activities have no explicit predecessors defined in the source data; agents must not infer dependencies from prose.
rule.rationale: Hallucinated dependencies produce incorrect critical paths and conceal missing information.

rule.statement: Use business-calendar libraries for all date arithmetic; never compute working days with raw date math.
rule.rationale: Raw date math silently skips weekends, public holidays, and half-days.

list.item: Pure agile teams with fixed-cadence sprints — velocity and roadmap replace CPM
Highly creative / R&D work where activity duration is unknowable
Tasks under 2 weeks with a single owner — a checklist suffices
Fixed-deadline projects where you back-plan from end date — use reverse-pass
